nativeRange.habitat: Intertidal and shallow subtidal rocky reefs, rubble zones, and macroalgae-rich rocky shores.

decorPreferences: well-secured live rock with grazing surfaces
glue or epoxy coral frags to rockwork to prevent toppling

diet.notes: The Turbo Snail is a voracious algae grazer, working across live rock and glass to consume hair algae, film algae, and diatoms. In systems where natural algae growth is limited, supplementing with dried seaweed (nori) clipped to the rockwork is generally advised to prevent starvation. Adequate calcium — around 400–450 ppm — supports shell maintenance.

avoidWith: large hermit crabs (will kill for the shell)

imageCandidates.notes: Alberto Alcala; ~1024x768. Live Turbo fluctuosus (Mexican Turbo) in a rocky tide pool, sharp and well-lit, showing the classic turban shell with brown/tan mottled-banded whorls. Best representative shot of the nominate species.

Frederic Ducarme; 4608x3456. Live Turbo petholatus (Tapestry Turbo) in situ on reef rock, crisp and vibrant, clearly showing the diagnostic iridescent green/orange 'cat's eye' operculum called out in the care notes.
Irene; 2048x1536. Live Turbo fluctuosus at low tide, sharp, showing the sealed green-tinged operculum spiral and encrusted shell from the aperture side - complementary angle to the recommended pick.

summary: The Turbo Snail is a powerful algae-grazing reef cleanup crew snail sold under several species in the trade. The large Mexican Turbo (Turbo fluctuosus) thrives in cooler water, while the Caribbean and Indo-Pacific congeners suit typical tropical reef temperatures.
careNotes:
This entry covers the Turbo congeners most commonly sold as reef cleanup-crew snails: Turbo fluctuosus (Mexican Turbo, eastern Pacific), Turbo brunneus / Turbo bruneus (Tropical or Banded Turbo, Indo-Pacific), Turbo castanea (Chestnut Turbo, Caribbean), and Turbo petholatus (Tapestry Turbo, Indo-Pacific). All are powerful herbivorous grazers that quickly clear hair algae, diatoms, and film algae from rock and glass.

Temperature dimorphism is the most important husbandry distinction in this group. The large Mexican Turbo originates in the cool upwelling waters of the Gulf of California and is generally advised to be kept around 20–24°C; sustained temperatures at or above 26°C are commonly reported to shorten its lifespan dramatically. The warmer-water Caribbean and Indo-Pacific congeners (T. brunneus/bruneus, T. castanea, T. petholatus) tend to do best at typical tropical reef temperatures of around 23–27°C. Aquarists keeping a mixed cleanup crew in a warm reef tank should generally favor the tropical species and avoid Mexican Turbos in tanks held above ~25°C year-round.

All Turbos are slow but powerful 'bulldozers' that can topple unsecured coral frags and rocks while grazing — glue frags down and stabilize rockwork. They are also persistent climbers and have been reported to crawl out of open-top aquariums; a tightly-fitting lid is strongly recommended, especially for rimless tanks. A flipped snail on bare substrate can struggle to right itself and may need to be turned over by hand. They are sensitive to copper-based medications and to rapid swings in salinity, pH, or temperature, and benefit from a slow drip acclimation.

Adult size in the trade typically falls in the 4–7 cm shell-diameter range, with the Mexican Turbo at the larger end and T. petholatus famously producing iridescent green 'cat's eye' opercula.
breedingNotes: Turbo snails are gonochoric broadcast spawners with planktonic larvae. Captive breeding in home reef aquaria is not practically achieved; commercial supply is wild-collected.

primary.notes: Primary product page for Turbo Snail (Mexican Turbo, Turbo fluctuosa). Provided scientific name, family, native range, water parameters, diet, reef-safe status, and care difficulty.

additional.name: Aquariumbreeder — Mexican Turbo Snail Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (up to ~7 cm), lifespan, cold-water preference (20–26°C, optimum 21–23°C), and frag-toppling/bulldozing behavior. Confirmed sensitivity to parameter swings.

additional.name: SaltwaterAquarium.com — Tropical Turbo Snail (Turbo bruneus)
additional.notes: Used to characterize the warmer-water Indo-Pacific Turbo congeners in the umbrella entry: 72–78°F (22–26°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, ~10 gallon minimum, reef-safe with frag-toppling risk. Substituted for Reef2Reef Wiki secondary which returned HTTP 403.

additional.name: Reef2Reef community threads (escape behavior)
additional.notes: Multiple Reef2Reef threads confirm Turbo snails regularly climb out of open-top tanks — supports climbsOutOfTank: true and the lid recommendation.
